creator: dewayne@heuristicsystems.com.au
text:
(In reply to Xin LI from comment #12)
I was initially very disappointed with mcrypt.  It kept using cast-128 on the i386 system and issued a "bus error" on the amd64, regardless of being built with clang or gcc5.4.  The platform is FreeBSD 11.1 Stable.

On the i386, mcrypt didn't do what I asked! For example:
# echo hi>z; Key2=`openssl rand 32 -hex`
# mcrypt  -V --keymode hex --key $Key2 --algorithm SERPENT --mode CBC  --hash md5 z
Warning: It is insecure to specify keywords in the command line
Algorithm serpent is not available in OpenPGP encryption.
cast-128 will be used instead.
Algorithm: cast-128
Keysize: 16
Mode: ncfb
Keyword mode: s2k-isalted-sha1
File format: openpgp
Input File: z
Output File: z.nc

Note that the algorithm, mode and keyword mode were not what I asked for. 

Eventually I got this to work with most of what I asked:

# mcrypt  -V --keymode hex --key $Key2 --algorithm SERPENT --mode CBC  --hash md5 --no-openpgp --bare  z
Warning: It is insecure to specify keywords in the command line
Algorithm: serpent
Keysize: 32
Mode: cbc
Keyword mode: hex
File format: bare
Input File: z
Output File: z.nc

and 
# hd z1.nc
00000000  ce f8 cf 0b 85 c9 47 cb  3b 7c 3c 76 27 89 98 b1  |......G.;|<v'...|
00000010  b7 3c 46 12 8b 16 e1 15  ca b3 58 32 26 1c b9 d6  |.<F.......X2&...|
00000020
Looks about right.  Though I doubt there's an md5 in there, most likely an embedded IV.  ;)

I also moved files between the i386 and the amd64 (encrypt/decrypt) all good. 

And for fun:
echo hi>z
cat z | mcrypt -q --force --keymode hex --key $Key1 --algorithm SERPENT --mode CBC --no-openpgp | mcrypt -q --force --keymode hex --key $Key2 --algorithm twofish --mode CBC --no-openpgp | mcrypt -d -q --force  --keymode hex --key $Key2 --algorithm twofish --mode CBC --no-openpgp | mcrypt -d -q --force --keymode hex --key $Key1 --algorithm SERPENT --mode CBC --no-openpgp
hi

also worked.  

I would strongly recommend reading both "man mcrypt" and "man 3 mcrypt" and --verbose is your friend, in this instance.

A password/key of 3 letters or less, probably should be reconsidered. Try to use the full key size so for a key of 256 bits, you'll need 32 "letters". ;)

So yes, mcrypt works but you really should verify, as it may not be doing what you think it is.  :)

And to close things out.  
# dd if=/dev/zero of=z bs=8m count=256; # 2G file.
# /usr/bin/time -l cat z| mcrypt -q --force  --key a  --algorithm $ALG --bare --noiv --no-openpgp --mode CBC  >/dev/null
where:
ALG= | realtime
saferplus 84 (repeated 84)
gost 65
serpent 36
twofish 21
rijndael 29
and for comparison cat z| openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -pass pass:a >/dev/null
took 5.8  (openssl was built with -O3, while mcrypt was -O2)
On a 1.8GHz, 8-core with starting vm.loadavg={ 0.08 0.11 0.20 }.
